title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, and directed staff to develop enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines starting at $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and consultant services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Report

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. The village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settlement issues, and Schofield Avenue punch list items with HOS Construction including manhole repairs and concrete work.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Spring activities included curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from East Nick, inlet repairs on Birch, and signing installation on Schofield Avenue. Jones Street culvert failure required installing a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed including removing plow equipment and servicing mowers and tractors.

Josh reported on service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration issues. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. The Kathleen lift station was identified as having significant clear water discharge, likely from sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers. In-pipe Technologies will conduct an INI study to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately $6.8 million in cash reserves. Approved unanimously on motion to acknowledge.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term state transportation funding solutions. Current state funding doesn't cover recurring costs like street maintenance and snow plowing, requiring general tax subsidies. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Committee member thanked Michael for grant work. Motion to recommend village board adopt resolution passed unanimously.

King Gravel submitted low bid of $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. Michael demonstrated savings of approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, saving about $130,000 across three 2026 projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fine amounts for illegal sump pump discharges to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee members discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Trustee Zagami suggested waiting for In-pipe Technologies study results, then sending violation letters with 30-60 day correction period. Committee agreed to start fines at $100/day. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language not exceeding $100/day passed unanimously.

A duplex builder at 2230 Radkkey Avenue needs temporary water/sewer connection via long laterals until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction extends mains through Outlot 1. Committee directed adding language that reconstruction occur no later than 2029. Motion to recommend agreement with assessment upon reconstruction passed unanimously.

Agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Rothschild holds contract with Steam Construction; Weston responsible for water ($88,704) and sanitary sewer ($6,156) totaling $94,860. Water main relocation needed due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Due to vacant engineering tech position, consultant proposals were sought for construction inspection. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; MSA recommended. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730; Clark Dietz recommended due to full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated total)
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines starting at $100/day
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Recommend village board approve Radkkey Avenue water/sewer connection agreement with reconstruction no later than 2029
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 in utility costs
Award Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services to MSA ($37,315)
Award Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz ($73,730)
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Staff to add street lighting to Schofield Avenue reconstruction project (Birch to Camp Phillips)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for Bladel Avenue - Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday this week

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by a member, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated for secretary, seconded, and approved unanimously.
The minutes from April 21st were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request that organizers provide better hotel room data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. The commission also scheduled the first steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update for June 1st.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: The commission reviewed the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Staff member Jen presented that the project meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to accommodate a gas pipeline easement, with the parking lot now positioned over the 36-inch deep gas line. Discussion covered future expansion possibilities to the north, placement of an alternate storage shed, and landscaping meeting current code. A motion was made to recommend approval to the board of trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5. The motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Jen presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and site drainage, and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling and containment. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Discussion revealed one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process. Commission agreed staff should reach out to that facility. A motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
The minutes from the April 13, 2026 meeting were approved as presented. Motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved items and building permits
discussions.body: Both the certified survey map/site plan report and building permit report for April 2026 were approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jen announced the first steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m., involving a joint Plan Commission and Village Board group. She will send calendar invites to members.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online participation. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous accommodations made for Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on salt shed inspections in wellhead protection zones, noting some zoning complaints have gone unaddressed for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with two annual inspections per facility
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that lacks conditional use approval
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be discussed at future meeting in fall

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, reviewing committee structure, jurisdiction, and receiving educational presentations from department heads. No action items were voted on except approval of April 1st meeting minutes. Department heads from Health, Veterans Services, and ADRC presented overviews of their operations and budgets.

agenda.item: Call to order and notation of remote participants (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations/functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Services Department presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging and Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried with all in favor by voice vote.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction over Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Services, North Central Health Care, and ADRC. He detailed reporting relationships with statutory boards including Social Services Board, Veterans Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, Board of Health, and NCH Board. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on developing a new strategic plan by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's 37.6 FTE staff across environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted public health vending machines providing free resources including tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, homeless system response efforts (new position funded by county and city), and radon/lead mitigation work. About half of the $5 million budget is tax levy, with environmental health designed to be self-supporting through licensing fees.

Administrator Leonard provided overview of the county's second largest department with nearly 140 FTE. He highlighted the Family Keys housing stability program piloted with state funding, which has been very successful addressing root causes of housing instability. The department has a $22 million revenue budget with approximately $7 million in tax levy. High-cost placements for youth can reach $600,000 annually.

VSO Erin Galindo reported the department increased to 3.0 FTE and handled 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900. Veterans in Marathon County received $2.6 million in retroactive VA payments and $44 million annually in total benefits. He highlighted the PACT Act's impact on presumptive conditions, surviving spouse benefits (DIC providing up to $2,400/month), housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). In response to Supervisor Kremes, Galindo explained regional collaboration with other county VSOs and noted all offices are undermanned statewide.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county regional model (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) serving aging and disability populations. He noted 11% of budget is county levy, with federal/state funding flat while nutrition program costs increased 40-45% over four years. The organization has about 60 FTE plus 385-400 volunteers. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. First levy increase request was in 2024 after being flat since 2011.

actionItems: Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggestions for future agenda items
Strategic plan development to be a focus over coming year with goal of completion by December

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bicycle regulations, and engaged in extensive discussion about farmers market parking concerns at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bicycles, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplex units, and to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the justification for the numbers, stating the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Malling spoke in favor, noting the fee is justified as new families require more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, and Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional inspection sticker system where food trucks inspected by any participating municipality (Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover Belt, Riverside, and Safer) don't need re-inspection in other participating communities. The ordinance passed unanimously.

Significant debate occurred over policy language. The original 2023 policy required health, safety, or welfare reasons for remote attendance. Trustees Beck, Olsen, and others argued the policy should match what was voted on at the last meeting - simply requiring notification, camera on, and no closed session participation, without requiring specific justifications. Trustee Beck moved to amend striking sentences about health/safety justifications. The amendment passed 5-2 with Trustees Herling and Malling voting no. The original motion as amended then passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Both appointments were approved unanimously. Parks, HR/Finance, and CLIPS still have openings. Board requested that committee applicant resumes be included in future packets.

Extended discussion on parking concerns at Kennedy Park farmers market location. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along the parking lot. Renee (staff) explained history: market grew from 25 stalls at old location to 42 at Camp Phillips/Schofield, now 53 spots at Kennedy Park where vendors can keep vehicles. Multiple trustees noted the market is very popular despite complaints. Tammach Miller Park was discussed but rejected as too far from traffic. No formal action taken; discussion only.

Public Works Director Michael addressed why outside engineering consultants are needed for project oversight at approximately $108,000 (as noted in public comment). He explained the engineering technician position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skillset is scarce in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms having difficulty finding local candidates.

Director Michael reported on the speed limit study for Weston Avenue. DOT engineering study showed it should be 35 mph, but the board previously directed keeping it at 45. Traffic data showed 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, with most traffic between 44-50 mph and only one vehicle under 35 mph. A second study is needed but construction delayed it. The matter will return to the board, possibly at a joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5202 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the decision to hire outside engineering consultants for $108,000 instead of filling the vacant engineering technician position. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed. He complained about lack of accountability for staff and insufficient information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet. No other public comment was offered.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective upon ordinance passage
Mobile food vendor regional inspection sticker system implemented
E-bicycle regulations ordinance adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand article dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit issue back to board or joint meeting
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle building inspections

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following the recent severe weather that impacted Ringle and other communities. Public speakers requested the county waive fees entirely for affected residents.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call and sign-in (technical difficulties with meeting manager)
Committee appointments and chair remarks on leadership
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster debris

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks emphasizing that 34% of the board is in its first or second term and explaining goals for committee assignments including increasing diversity of perspectives and mentoring opportunities. Vice chairs were tasked with shepherding the strategic plan. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who praised her advocacy for 'smallest children and oldest elders'), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher explained the proposed fee reduction for disaster debris from the April severe weather. The standard $65/ton rate would be reduced to approximately $36.94 for certified disaster debris, reflecting waivers from the state ($13.10) and Town of Ringle ($2.33), plus a county reduction to commercial rates. Leonard noted the county will not meet the $670,743 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle to qualify. Public speakers Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck requested the county waive fees entirely and extend relief beyond 60 days for the approximately 44 affected homes. Supervisor Rosenberg supported the work but requested continued discussion. The board suspended rules unanimously to consider the resolution, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

publicComment: Special dispensation was granted for two speakers on the landfill tipping fee item. Jessica Toasty stated that the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving fees and asked Marathon County to do the same, noting residents are self-reliant and private businesses have donated $40,000+ in cleanup efforts. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 homeowners facing demolition, noting many insurance adjusters have not yet arrived and requesting extended fee relief beyond 60 days.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approved increasing out of home placement reserve
Resolution 25-26 approved amending social services budget
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for uninsured costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Board to revisit tipping fee relief at May meeting if extension beyond state declaration period is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver consideration for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff recommendations for temporarily waiving county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Eastston, villages of Cronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about potential levy impacts. Supervisor Drebeck questioned fairness compared to individual disasters not declared emergencies. Eric Vogel asked about insurance coverage. After extensive debate, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution to the county board. Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to only waive reconnect fees for septic systems (not other zoning or septic fees). Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed. The amended motion to forward to county board passed, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an educational overview of solid waste operations, including the landfill history since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations requiring treatment by 2032, and upcoming capital projects totaling millions of dollars. Marilyn Bend praised the department for keeping private landfill costs in check.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He covered the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees, 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and services provided to City of Wausau. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. No one signed up for public comment at the beginning of the meeting, and no members of the public spoke during the rezoning public hearings.
actionItems: Both rezoning petitions forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for final approval
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) forwarded to county board for consideration
Towns should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable
Staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Conservation services presentation to be scheduled for next meeting with Kirsty Heiden

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of kayak launch signs for the Plover River and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the absent chair can participate. The meeting also included updates on Kennedy Park fundraising progress and discussion of graffiti removal challenges on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail

discussions.body: Minutes from April 27th, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Director Sean Oster shared a timeline document tracking the Kennedy Park project from the comprehensive plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Scott asked about village capital funds earmarked for Kennedy Park, and Oster clarified that no funds are currently earmarked as those items were pulled pending the master plan. The Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for field lights, having approximately $85,000 of the $117,000 goal at the community foundation. A public presentation of funds raised will occur at their June tournament. The presiding member defended the Friends group, noting they are volunteers who should be given grace and that master plans are guiding documents subject to change.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff provided photos and a PDF showing the approximate location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during construction. The mats are 8x8 timbers owned by the village, with extras potentially available for trail projects. TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR Pipeline) inspected the work and was present during mat installation.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month because Katrina, who requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy from 6504 [address unclear] spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The presiding member noted Andy's objection would be recorded and invited him to return next month.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting access limitations prevent bringing a pressure washer to the site. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and organizing volunteer workdays. Committee member Laro suggested community volunteers might help with seal coating. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options and report back.

Dan Knoeck from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of proposed kayak launch signs for the Plover River. Three signs will be installed in Weston at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks launches. Signs are funded through Marathon County Parks Foundation grants with no cost to the village. The Highway J launch will likely be closed this season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve village installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in adding food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds, noting an outside group offered to provide signs free of charge. Committee agreed to add it to a future agenda for further discussion. The Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler consultants next Tuesday.

publicComment: Two residents provided public comment. Andy from 6504 [street inaudible] spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (appearing online as 'Vern M' due to system defaults) from 5002 [Street] Weston criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 figure for equipment 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and glow event proceeds are being tracked. He suggested the Friends of Kennedy Park may be 'frauds' for not sharing detailed financials.
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June 22nd meeting
Staff to explore graffiti removal and prevention options for Oaklair River Trail
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at three Weston locations - to be forwarded to Village Board
Food allergy awareness signage topic to be added to future agenda
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved minutes and acknowledged financial reports, then received a detailed educational presentation from the Municipal Clerk's office covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledge work product transmittals - April budget status report
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR Committee meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. Motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

A committee member asked about the final numbers for TIF closeout. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, noting the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds which are due October 1st. Staff confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented a comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations, including staffing (now 2 full-time and 1 part-time employee, down from 4 full-time previously), elections administration, licensing, tax collection, and the village newsletter. Key points included: the office handled 84% of tax collection ($22 million) at the counter; they have 51 liquor licenses; pet licensing was brought back in-house saving the $5,495 previously paid to a third-party vendor; the quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually in postage and printing. Discussion included the increase in pet licensing fees from $10 to $20 for neutered animals to help cover Marathon County Humane Society costs, and concerns about the cost of the non-dog stray contract including $175 per mouse for eight mice collected.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well over the past three Saturdays, with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, and craft people. Vendors expressed they were very happy with the setup and location. Committee members raised concerns about parking congestion on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying parking is now allowed on the west side of Alderson while the east side remains no parking.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged outreach to potential citizen participants.

actionItems: CDA resolution to call TIF bonds anticipated this summer for October payoff
Recruit citizens to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance code, recommended an e-bike ordinance prohibiting emotos on roadways/sidewalks, authorized fire inspections for mobile food vendors, and approved a raze order for a deteriorated structure. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both items for further review.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Discussion on proposed amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances
Discussion on proposed e-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82
Discussion on crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Amendment to mobile food vendors license for fire inspections

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after property sale and permit submission. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work with zoning during summer for violations and pet licensing. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 made by Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, passed unanimously.

Committee reviewed final version of nuisance code amendments, including tree limb heights over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet). Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees made by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion on emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles). Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks due to safety concerns, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee debated whether to restrict electric scooters used by children. Chief Hunt noted emotos are not yet covered by state statute. Motion to recommend approval with addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property made by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM to 4 PM) is far outside other municipalities' 5-hour windows. DPW Director Michael Wodalski suggested eliminating village-wide overnight parking restrictions and instead signing 6-8 specific problem areas. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking, noting it gives officers time to clear cars before plowing. Committee deferred action, asking Wodalski to identify specific exception areas for the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot. DPW estimated $2,000-$3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-$30,000 for lighted system. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for private benefit. Motion made by Olsen to recommend approval only if Mount Olive covers the cost, seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously. Staff will contact Mount Olive about cost-sharing.

discussions.item: Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance
discussions.body: Fire Chief explained new regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston (Wausau opted out). Food trucks inspected once get sticker valid for all participating municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane cylinders. Motion to approve Ordinance 26-8 made by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing second address), 3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence with apartment), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt noted additional addressing issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski. Motion to recommend approval made, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Structure abandoned since 2010-2011, on enforcement list since 2021. Fire department previously declined training burn due to unsafe conditions. Owners discovered housing chickens inside in previous years. Property currently for sale. Demolition costs will be added to tax roll. Motion to recommend Board authorize raze order made by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass destruction from vehicle parking and questioning ADA compliance of handicap spaces on grass rather than asphalt. He questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks. He raised concerns about food truck regulations on public property and unclear farmers market day definitions. He also criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, stating some have been on the list for years with no visible progress on at least three he personally knows about.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for May agenda
Recommend e-bike ordinance to Board of Trustees with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
DPW Director Wodalski to identify 6-8 specific areas for overnight parking restrictions and bring back in July/August
Staff to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees
Recommend Board authorize raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue
Investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber for potential code violation (furniture and dumpster outside)
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Economic Development Committee Meeting
overview: The Economic Development Committee held its organizational meeting for the 2026-2028 term, with elections for chairperson and vice chairperson and establishment of a regular meeting schedule. The committee also considered deed restriction changes for 725 S. 84th Avenue and received an overview of the Community Development Department.

Establish regular meeting date and time for the 2026-2028 term
Consideration of April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee Minutes
Approving Consent to Transfer, Waiver of Right of First Refusal, Termination of Deed Restrictions and Approval of New Deed Restrictions for 725 S. 84th Avenue

discussions.body: The committee held elections for leadership positions for the new 2026-2028 term. Specific vote counts and election results are not recorded in the official vote records.

The committee considered the minutes from the April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee meeting. The outcome of any approval vote is not recorded in the official vote records.

The committee received a discussion item providing an overview of the Community Development Department. This was an informational item with no action required.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session to hold a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to consider action on a pupil expulsion matter during or following the closed session hearing

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects

Mayoral appointments confirmed to Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board
Amendments to Ordinance 02-0432 as stated by Alder Watson and Alder Tierney to be reviewed by City Attorney
